sentences.text: My hands were severely broken fingers and twisted out of proportion, as you can see.
They are not hands of arthritic condition.
There were no medical attention taken during this period of time.
But I remember that the cold floor and wet floor of the cell helped me somehow to recover and to cope with it.
And then--
--with time, it healed.
This was through the beatings of-- during the interrogation of the-- by the gestapo in Aleja Szucha in 1941.
By asking questions about certain activities, and certain people.
I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] could not answer, and pleaded with them that I don't know them, don't know anything about it.
So they were beating me about the face, kicking me in groin, and stepping on my fingers while I was laying on the floor, and pulling it from under-- the hands from under their boots.
And that's how my finger was broken.
It was not a simple situation.
And I somehow look at my fingers today and say, these are not arthritic fingers, they are strong hands.
But nevertheless, they are absolutely out of form.
I'm grateful that I didn't lose any of them.
When it comes to the life in the prison, there was quite simple.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [BACKROUND NOISE] It was routine conducting daily life by being waken up by a bell, and a sort of-- sort of very short wash up, and distribution of a little food.
And then outside duties of cleaning the yards, or marching, or conducting some orders.
And there were also people who were working in a tailor shop and in mechanical shops.
There were quite old prisoners staying in prison over a year, and they were privileged with these jobs.
We-- there were Jews in prison, and they were identified by a yellow triangle they were wearing on their chests.
And they had to respond to the number.
I don't remember the number of my [? staying ?] at Pawiak, but I do remember that there were all kinds of people, and primarily people between the ages of 25 and 45, young Polish people.
And among them, there were some priests.
And we knew that they were priests because they were allowed to conduct services on Sunday for the other prisoners.
There were some Jewish prisoners.
[BACKROUND NOISE] And as I mentioned, it was easy to identify them because of the yellow triangle they were wearing on the left side of the chest.
There was no such thing as celebration in anything, not even, not the only holiday.
But you were celebrating because you were getting up the next morning.
And coming back from my interrogation, Aleja Szucha from the gestapo headquarter.
One day, I was very, very fortunate that they just grabbed me out-- out of the cell.
I did not go for that day for interrogation.
And there was a group of youngsters already working in outside, and they were shipping coal.
In other words, transporting coal from the-- from the railroad tracks to the gestapo headquarter.
There was on a daily basis done constantly, without stop.
This kind of work took part from 7 o'clock in the morning until 11 o'clock at night.
From time to time, they were [? feeling ?] [? out ?] quite well, but nevertheless our backs were breaking on us, to the point that we couldn't just straighten out.
That's how I got out of Pawiak.
There was a miracle.
And then one day, the entire group was sent to Majdanek in early 1942.
I was about, in Pawiak about two months.
And when I got out, I went to this working group.
They were allowed to return to ghetto, to their home, once a week.
They were [INAUDIBLE] they-- they kept them all week long sleeping in the abnormal conditions, in dilapidated, bombed-out barracks of the former Polish-- Polish Army quarters.
And on the weekend, they allowed the entire group to return together.
And when I was wondering, is this-- is this, am I included?
And when I asked the foreman, he said to me, you silly fool.
You don't questions, you just do it.
That's how I got back to the ghetto.
And when I got to ghetto, I-- then they want the [INAUDIBLE] that we had on Sunday.
I contacted the people who could deliver the word through [INAUDIBLE] what happened to me.
And we-- he came to visit me very shortly and find out what's going on.
And he said, we can't sacrifice anything of getting you out of there.
Let's see how that will work for another month.
But unfortunately, a month later, after I got for the first time-- after a long time not seeing [? mother, ?] there was [INAUDIBLE] in 1942, early even [INAUDIBLE].
It was sometime in April or end of April.
And they took us to Majdanek.
There's an entire column of these people who were working, shipping coal to Majdanek.
And we were came through Majdanek directly from on their trucks, with their coal, not expecting any tricks.
And they left us there for the-- a SS [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to take care of us.
And--
Drove away.

There were a number of people, youngsters, who are in about my age, or a little older, that came to work on a-- working for somebody else or were caught by the gestapo and forced to come every day to work.
They were all Jews.
They were all Jews, and there were 28 of them if I'm not mistaken.
And they were brought to Majdanek and were put in a working column the following day, were building dirt field, that means the first field.
And the second field were already built.
The field was a separate compound, separate [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] piece of land, on which another camp, a camp in a camp.
And in this camp, there were 22 barracks, plus the administration barracks and the other facilities, including a kitchen, and the bathrooms, and things like this.
In each share of these barracks were between 300 and 400 people, to 500.
It became really congested.
And there were daily selections going on.

Required a great number of Russian prisoner of war, which were dying left and right because of malnutrition and impossible conditions of-- They kept to themselves and did not associate with anybody else.
And during the late months of 1942, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] he sent us to work into the Lublin, to the city of Lublin, which was about 10 kilometers from Majdanek.
Yes, yes.
Just carrying building materials from place to place and putting it in place.
Most of the walls for the barracks building material, building material for barracks on the dirt field.
When we ran out of the material, then sent us to work in a munition factory in Lublin.
And in the--
Until they sent us to Lublin.
This was May, June, July, August, September, and October.
And October, end of October they sent us to Lublin.
They ran out of material, so they didn't have any work for us in a camp.
And they took these people who they considered a qualified workforce, and they sent us to the munition factory in Lublin and-- and in the aircraft factory in Lublin.
Very in the initial stages.
They were already experimenting with the crematoriums, and many of the Russian soldiers, were-- the POWs were dying of malnutrition, but they were trying to cremate them.
And that's where the crematorium started to function.
Yes.
There was a very specific smell when they were using a crematorium that can't be described by a human being.
The smell of burning flesh [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that could cause many unpleasant feelings of nausea and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] causing a trauma.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We knew what was going on, because where we were coming to receive the food in line, the kapos would say, if you won't behave, you will go like them, and pointing out with their face to-- to the chimneys of the crematorium.
And we knew that they meant what they said.

There were no-- a kapo was actually [INAUDIBLE] police, but this was people who were chosen by the Germans to conduct certain functions for administrative purposes of controlling people and, if necessary, beating them up to a point of no return.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then we-- while I was working in the [INAUDIBLE] group that I went to work with in Lublin was coming daily back and forth.
Working with other groups.
And there were German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in uniform who were erecting this barrack.
And many of our people who had any knowledge of building industry were engaged in it, and they were helping them out.
And when the material was there, there was work going on 24 hours a day, two shifts, 12 hours a shift.
I was working during the day, and the shift was from 6 o'clock in the morning till 6 o'clock at night.
Then there was the night shift, what they call this, from 6 o'clock in the evening till 6 o'clock in the morning.
And a great amount of fatalities among our people were during the night shift, where people simply couldn't conduct themselves on a basis of delivering whatever was asked of them.
And the kapo, if not the kapo, then the Germans took advantage of them, like hit them to a point of no, that they lost their consciences.
And they were carried away to be liquidated.
The guards were awful.
They were always walking around with a whip in their hand, or with an iron bar.
So if they didn't like any of your movement, they just hit you and hit you to the point that they could break either your bones or your back.
Or if they hit you in the head, they were definitely knocking the brain out of you.
No.
I was fortunate that I, I was somehow fast enough for them not to be hit or be molested.
Yes, a SS man who did not like people who were-- who did not walk fast enough, especially when there was rainy days and they had the clogs, the Dutch shoes out of wood.
And they couldn't move fast enough.
Then they would just take a pistol, they said, you're of no use to us, and didn't even come to a close distance, but just shot him.
Yes, several times.
And of course, you got scared, and you realized that you could be next.
Very unpleasant situation that you were living from day to day, and wondering what was better, to die or to be alive.
Somehow, many of us did not have the courage to take our own life by jumping on the wires of the separating one field from the other, which were loaded with a high voltage current.
There were people who couldn't stand it and jumped on the wire.
They were electrified-- electrocuted immediately.
And the fun of it, the Germans said, was to engage other people to pull them off the wire.
And the minute you touched it, you got the shock.
And some of us had learned that when they asked us to remove the body from the wires, we took our tunic off to protect our hands.
And that's how we pulled them off the wire.
Tunic.
The part of the uniform.
No, I did not.
You-- you had-- you did not have any contact with the Russian POWs.
They were in separate barracks, and Polish Jews were in separate barracks, too.
And the only thing that was on our mind, our course of conversation was food or dreaming about food.
Whatever we were getting was very, very precious to us, and what-- whatever we were getting was not enough for us.
And we knew that the days of our existence is coming to an end, because we could not exist under this condition for very long.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How did we-- there was a certain amount of hope in us, especially when we heard the airplanes passing by and there was a raid.
Then we knew that there were Russian planes coming over on the-- on-- on the-- on the way to bombard something.
And this was the only few minutes of rest that we really had.
And it came that the sleeping was permitted the minute the lights were out at 9 o'clock.
At 4 o'clock in the morning, they get you-- got you out on a place to be counted and be prepared for the day shift to work on.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Condition of living in the barracks were awful, and we had lice that were eating us alive.
And there was no hope of being able to get rid of it.
Many people were sitting at night and just killing lice in their uniform, because they couldn't stand it anymore.
And many with the lice of course came the typhus epidemic and decimated our ranks without any-- without any mercy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we went to work--
They knew very well that the only way to survive is to-- to support one another, extend the good word of-- of friendship.
There was no fighting among the prisoners.
Especially when you encounter the hanging of a prisoner, and especially conducted on Sundays.
For what reason that they?
It didn't matter what reason it was.
The Germans declared this individual guilty, and they hung him.
They-- we had to witness this, standing on appelplatz, after we returned from work after half a day.
On Sunday, we were working half a day.
Not that the Germans were so gracious to give us half a day, but they needed some rest for themself, too.
And so they did not force us to work on half a day on Sunday.
The only free time that we had was between Sunday 12 o'clock until the evening.
And you were sitting outside, and there were all kind of things to be conducted, like shaving, or sharing, barber was active.
And of course, if he didn't like your face, he was shaving you with a razor that was very dull.
But if he did like you, then he sharpened the razor.
There was no soap, or anything like that, to wash yourself with, and things like this.
The only soap that I remember was given to us when we went to [INAUDIBLE], and there was maybe once in a month.
The funny part of it was on the soap was written R-I-S, which we were translating it as [SPEAKING YIDDISH], pure Jewish soap.
Whether that was true or not, I-- we could not tell.
But it was the substance of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very sandy without any true value of a soap.
But somehow, we managed to wash yourself [INAUDIBLE] leave that we had during this hot days and some cold days.
It is very hard to describe to people what it means not to be able to take a shower, never mind every day, but at least once a week.
How can a human being survive?
Well, we did survive somehow.
And we overcame this situation with hygiene, and we're very fortunate.
It is not easy to describe what food we had.
We had a small piece of bread and a little bit of coffee in the morning.
And there were, we were fortunate we got some little marmalade, or at 12 o'clock, when we got the soup there was watery substance, with leaves and a few potatoes floating.
On the way back from work at 6 o'clock, and we got another portion of soup.
And you were fortunate if you were one of the very first ones to have this soup distributed when the few potatoes were floating.
Or if they did like or did not like your face.
Is very hard to describe what-- what feeling you had when you looked at the soap.
You didn't know whether to wash yourself with it, or-- or to swallow it and eat it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
How did people survive?
By encouraging each other, and giving [? each other ?] hope that tomorrow might be the end of the war, or maybe in a week, or so.
We knew that we had great hope.
That perhaps what happened at the beginning of 1941, when the Germans attacked Russia, that we knew about it already at the time, that this would not-- How did you know?
--prevail.
Well, when we were working in-- for the Germans, and like [INAUDIBLE], we had crossing by a truck the streets of Warsaw.
We knew that the Russian prisons are being marched daily to show the population how did they look, how the Great Red Army looked.
And there was a devastating sight of poorly dressed and with long coats, unusual hats.
Majority of them were of Asian [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] origin, like Mongols, Uzbeks, and [INAUDIBLE].
And the shoes that they were wearing, they were not shoes.
They were sort of a-- a combination [INAUDIBLE] made out of material, not the leather.
And of course, the Germans were saying to us, take a look what a big army we-- we are fighting.
No wonder we got it so quickly.
Of course, all this, we knew that it was a great propaganda for the Germans to-- to exploit the people and make them believe that a new regime, a new era is coming for them to experience.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one day we were already in camp, and we realized that something must have been happening because they needed the workforce.
And they start taking from the camps people for work.
And this started, actually, in August of 1943.
I beg--
When the material for the dirt field ran out, then they sent us to the munition factory in Lublin.
And the supervisors in this munition factory and in-- and in the aircraft factory were Polish people from the city of Lublin.
And from them, we got the news that-- what's going on on the frontiers, and so on.
Some of the Germans were, some are killing us by saying, well, I wonder how well you gonna take care of us when we're gonna lose the war.
And we were surprised to hear, because never ever before, even from the elder Germans, we could hear words like this.
And when I was in munition factory, somebody contact me.

And the only way, I mentioned once before, that I had responded to the word [NON-ENGLISH] when somebody [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] mentioned to me the word [NON-ENGLISH], I looked and I didn't pay attention.
And then a Polish man came over to me and said, do you know what [NON-ENGLISH] is?
And I still looked at him, I didn't answer him, I kept going, doing my work.
Pushing a little cart with ammunition from place to place, loading and unloading.
Until one day, one of these men, when we are sitting and eating lunch.
And it was not lunch like on [INAUDIBLE] lunch in-- on Sixth Avenue.
There was nothing to eat but your fingernails.
And this man approached me face to face.
He said, I know that you are [NON-ENGLISH].
And then he handed me half of his sandwich.
And he said, I will help you out.
Just say [NON-ENGLISH].
I accepted in good faith, and didn't answer him, and kept going.
After the lunch, and the following day, he left again half of a sandwich.
And then I realized that there must be either a trick or-- or something else going on that I don't understand.
And then he told me that he is from the party, and he will do everything possible to arrange for me to escape.
